title: Baingan Bharta

overview.description: A smoky North Indian aubergine dish made by roasting the aubergine until soft and charred before cooking it with onion, tomato, ginger, garlic and spices.

method: Heat the oven grill to high or preheat the oven to 230°C.
Pierce the aubergines several times with a fork.
Rub the aubergines lightly with oil.
Place them under the grill or in the hot oven.
Cook, turning occasionally, until the skin is deeply charred and the flesh is completely soft.
This may take approximately 30–45 minutes depending on the cooking method and size of the aubergines.
Place the cooked aubergines in a bowl and cover for 10 minutes.
When cool enough to handle, remove the charred skin.
Roughly chop or mash the soft aubergine flesh.
Heat the remaining oil in a large frying pan.
Add the cumin seeds.
Add the onion and cook until softened and lightly golden.
Add the ginger, garlic and green chilli if using.
Cook for 1–2 minutes.
Add the tomatoes, turmeric, ground coriander, chilli powder and salt.
Cook until the tomatoes break down and the oil begins to separate from the masala.
Add the roasted aubergine flesh.
Cook gently for 10–15 minutes, stirring occasionally.
Stir through the lemon juice if using.
Finish with fresh coriander and serve.

learning.did-you-know: The name baingan bharta refers to aubergine that has been mashed or roughly crushed after being cooked, with the smoky flavour from roasting being a defining characteristic.
learning.what-you-will-learn: How high heat transforms aubergine and how charring vegetables can create deep smoky flavour without adding extra ingredients.

notes.why-its-in-the-chapman-kitchen: A vegetable dish that demonstrates how a single ingredient can be transformed through high-heat cooking and introduces the children to smoky, charred flavours.
chapman-tips: The aubergine must be completely soft before removing it from the heat.
Deep charring gives the best smoky flavour, but do not worry if the skin does not blacken perfectly evenly.
Covering the cooked aubergine makes the skin easier to remove.
Don't discard every dark piece of flesh; some of the smoky roasted flavour comes from the parts closest to the skin.
The finished dish should retain some texture rather than becoming completely smooth.
If the aubergine tastes slightly bitter, a little extra lemon juice can help balance it.
A large aubergine is usually easier to roast than several small ones.
notes.batch-cooking: Baingan bharta freezes well. Cool completely before freezing in airtight portions. Thaw overnight in the refrigerator and reheat gently in a pan.
notes.leftovers: Use as a filling for wraps, spread onto toast or serve with eggs.
